title: The line before everything
steps.0.text: Add `<!DOCTYPE html>` as the first line of the file, before `<html>`.
checkLabels.t4: The page name is still there
why.empty: The editor is empty. You are adding one line, and you deleted the whole page. Bring the code back.
hint2.empty: Restore the skeleton from the last lesson and put the declaration at the very top.
why.not-closed: Some tag is open and never closed, or closed in the wrong order.
hint2.not-closed: The declaration is not a tag and does not close. Check the rest.
why.no-doctype: There is no declaration. The browser switches to quirks mode — the one for sites from twenty-five years ago.
hint2.no-doctype: The first line of the file. Before `<html>`, not inside it.
hint3.no-doctype: The exact line is `<!DOCTYPE html>`. Look at the example.
why.lost-title: The page name is gone. You are adding one line, not deleting old ones.
why.lost-content: Something disappeared from the page. You are adding a line at the top — the body stays untouched.
hint2.lost-content: Compare with the starting code. The heading and the sentence belong in `<body>`.
blocks.1.text: Open any site at all. Right-click, View Page Source. The first line is the same everywhere — on Google, on the news site, at your bank.
blocks.2.text: It is not a formality and not a tradition. It solves a real problem you never see, because everybody writes it.
blocks.5.text: Add it to your page and run it. You will see no difference. That is exactly why most courses explain it wrongly — there is nothing to show.
blocks.7.text: You will meet the explanation "it tells the browser this is HTML5". That is not true. Older standards did give this line such a meaning, but no browser has ever used it for anything other than one single thing.
blocks.8.text: It is a switch with two positions. Nothing more.
blocks.10.text: The nineties: pages were written in two versions — one for Netscape Navigator, one for Internet Explorer. When the standards arrived, browsers could not simply adopt them, because that would break almost everything on the web.
blocks.11.text: So they introduced two modes. With the declaration — the rules from the standard. Without it — an imitation of Navigator 4 and Internet Explorer 5. Today there are three modes: quirks, limited-quirks and no-quirks, but the idea is the same.
blocks.13.text: The most tangible difference is how sizes are counted. Take a box:
blocks.15.text: In standards mode `width` is the content only — the padding and border are added on top and the box comes out 350 pixels wide. In quirks mode those same 300 include everything and the content shrinks.
blocks.16.text: So the page does not crash. It just has different sizes than you expect, and the cause is one missing line at the top. That is why, when a layout behaves inexplicably, you check the declaration before you go hunting through the CSS.
blocks.18.text: In the console, type `document.compatMode`. The answer `CSS1Compat` means everything is fine. `BackCompat` means you are in quirks mode.
blocks.19.text: Firefox even logs a warning to the console on its own when a page is in that mode. No warning means no problem.
blocks.21.text: Add the declaration to your page.
blocks.22.text: One rule: it is the FIRST thing in the file. Nothing before it — not a comment, not a blank line, not a space.
blocks.24.items.0: `<!DOCTYPE html>` is the first line of every HTML file.
blocks.24.items.1: It does not declare a version — it switches the browser's mode.
blocks.24.items.2: Without it, layout imitates browsers from the nineties.
blocks.24.items.3: The most visible effect is that sizes are counted differently.
blocks.24.items.4: You check it with `document.compatMode` in the console.
walkthrough.0.text: One line at the top. Nothing else is touched.
walkthrough.1.text: The capitals are not required — `<!doctype html>` works just as well. It is written in capitals by habit, to set it apart from tags. The check ignores case.
walkthrough.2.text: Notice there is no closing tag. This is not an element and it wraps nothing — it is an instruction to the browser, before the document has begun.
